tldr: Pinecone is a managed vector DB; Typesense is a typo-tolerant keyword search engine with optional vector search. Migration is only viable if your use case is product/doc search rather than pure semantic similarity. Re-index source documents into Typesense collections; keep embeddings only if you need hybrid/vector search.
why_switch: Open-source core — self-host or use managed cloud, no vendor lock-in
Lower entry price and simpler billing for small/medium workloads
Built-in typo tolerance, faceting, and ranking out of the box
InstantSearch / Algolia-compatible adapter for fast frontend wiring
Avoid proprietary vector format — store source documents, re-embed if needed
what_to_export: Source documents (the original text/records you embedded)
Vector embeddings per record (if reusing for hybrid search)
Per-record IDs to preserve referential integrity
Sparse vectors / hybrid search config (if used)
export_method: Pinecone has no full-index dump. Use the official client to list/fetch vectors in batches (fetch by ID or paginate via metadata filters), or — preferred — re-export from the original source-of-truth (DB, CMS, S3) that produced the embeddings.
import_method: Create a Typesense collection with a schema matching your fields; for hybrid search add a float[] field with the embedding dimension. Bulk-import via the documents import API (JSONL). Use Typesense's built-in embedding generation if you want it to manage vectors.

concept_mapping.target: Document with float[] field + scalar fields

concept_mapping.note: Combines with keyword search for hybrid

effort: 1-3 weeks for a small/medium app; longer if hybrid relevance tuning is critical
gotchas: Not a drop-in replacement: pure ANN/semantic-only workloads may not fit Typesense's keyword-first model
Recall and ranking will differ — budget time for relevance tuning and A/B testing
No built-in namespace primitive; plan multi-tenant isolation explicitly
Embedding dimensions are fixed at collection creation — schema changes mean re-indexing
Self-hosted ops (snapshots, HA, scaling) are on you unless you use Typesense Cloud
Cost profile flips above the free tier — model your record count and QPS before committing

email.subject: Moving our search from Pinecone to Typesense
email.body:
Hi team,

I'm planning to migrate our search workload from Pinecone to Typesense. Main drivers: open-source core, lower lock-in, simpler pricing at our scale, and built-in typo tolerance/faceting that fits most of our queries.

Plan: re-export source documents from our system of record, define a Typesense collection schema, bulk-import via the documents API, and run shadow queries to compare relevance before cutover. We'll keep embeddings for hybrid search where semantic recall matters.

Risks I'm tracking: relevance tuning differences, no native namespaces, and self-host vs. Typesense Cloud trade-off. Targeting 1-3 weeks end-to-end.

Happy to walk through the cutover plan.
